Solving binomial Thue equations

István Gaál  and László Remete
University of Debrecen, Mathematical Institute
H–4010 Debrecen Pf.12., Hungary
e–mail: igaal@science.unideb.hu, remetel42@gmail.com

This work was partially supported by the European Union and the European Social Fund through project Supercomputer, the national virtual lab (grant no.: TAMOP-4.2.2.C-11/1/KONV-2012-0010) and also supported in part by K67580 and K75566 from the Hungarian National Foundation for Scientific Research,
Abstract

We consider binomial Thue equations of type xn−m​yn=±1superscript𝑥𝑛𝑚superscript𝑦𝑛plus-or-minus1x^{n}-my^{n}=\pm 1 in x,y∈ℤ𝑥𝑦ℤx,y\in{\mathbb{Z}}. Optimizing the method of Pethő [8] we perform an extensive calculation by a high performance computer to determine all solutions with max⁡(|x|,|y|)<10500𝑥𝑦superscript10500\max(|x|,|y|)<10^{500} of binomial Thue equations for m<107𝑚superscript107m<10^{7} for exponents n=3,4,5,7,11,13,17,19,23,29𝑛3457111317192329n=3,4,5,7,11,13,17,19,23,29.

††2010 Mathematics Subject Classification: Primar 11D59; Secondary 11Y50††Key words and phrases: binomial Thue equations, supercomputers

1 Introduction

The method of Pethő [8] (see also [5]) gives a fast algorithm to calculate ”small” solutions of Thue equations. The method is based on the continued fraction algorithm. By ”small” solutions we mean those with absolute values less than, say 10500superscript1050010^{500}. Nobody belives that such equations have larger solutions. All our experiences show that such equations usually only have a few very small solutions.

In this paper we make this algorithm more efficient in order to calculate ”small” solutions of a special type of Thue equations, the binomial Thue equations of type

xn−m​yn=±1​in​x,y∈ℤ.formulae-sequencesuperscript𝑥𝑛𝑚superscript𝑦𝑛plus-or-minus1in𝑥𝑦ℤx^{n}-my^{n}=\pm 1\;\;{\rm in}\;\;x,y\in{\mathbb{Z}}. (1)

Using sharp estimates and utilizing the specialities of these equations we perform an extensive calculation by a high performance computer to determine solutions with max⁡(|x|,|y|)<10500𝑥𝑦superscript10500\max(|x|,|y|)<10^{500} of binomial Thue equations for 1<m<1071𝑚superscript1071<m<10^{7} (assuming that the left hand side is irreducible) for the exponents n=3,4,5,7,11,13,17,19,23,29𝑛3457111317192329n=3,4,5,7,11,13,17,19,23,29. Our data contains all solutions of these equations with high probability. These results complete several results on the solutions of binomial Thue equations [3], [2], [7].

2 Sharper estimates

Let n𝑛n be one of 3,5,7,11,13,17,19,23,293571113171923293,5,7,11,13,17,19,23,29, in fact our arguments are valid for any odd primes (the case n=4𝑛4n=4 we shall deal with later). Assume that for m𝑚m the left hand side of (1) is irreducible (we skip those m𝑚m for which the left hand side of (1) is reducible). Our purpose is to determine all solutions x,y∈ℤ𝑥𝑦ℤx,y\in{\mathbb{Z}} of (1) with max⁡(|x|,|y|)<C𝑥𝑦𝐶\max(|x|,|y|)<C. We shall perform our calculation with C=10500𝐶superscript10500C=10^{500}.

Let ζ=exp⁡(2​π​i/n)𝜁2𝜋𝑖𝑛\zeta=\exp(2\pi i/n). Let x,y𝑥𝑦x,y be and arbitrary solution of (1). Set

βj=x−ζj−1​mn​ysubscript𝛽𝑗𝑥superscript𝜁𝑗1𝑛𝑚𝑦\beta_{j}=x-\zeta^{j-1}\sqrt[n]{m}\ y

for j=1,…,n𝑗1…𝑛j=1,\ldots,n, then equation (1) can be written as

β1​…​βn=±1.subscript𝛽1…subscript𝛽𝑛plus-or-minus1\beta_{1}\ldots\beta_{n}=\pm 1. (2)

We may assume y≥0𝑦0y\geq 0 since on the right side we have ±1plus-or-minus1\pm 1 in our equation. Also, for y=0𝑦0y=0 we only have the trivial solution x=±1𝑥plus-or-minus1x=\pm 1. Therefore in the following let y≥1𝑦1y\geq 1.

For n≠4𝑛4n\neq 4 the β2,…,βnsubscript𝛽2…subscript𝛽𝑛\beta_{2},\ldots,\beta_{n} are complex, therefore

|βj|=|x−ζj−1​mn​y|≥|Im​(ζj−1)|​mn​ysubscript𝛽𝑗𝑥superscript𝜁𝑗1𝑛𝑚𝑦Imsuperscript𝜁𝑗1𝑛𝑚𝑦|\beta_{j}|=|x-\zeta^{j-1}\sqrt[n]{m}\ y|\geq|{\rm Im}(\zeta^{j-1})|\ \sqrt[n]{m}\ y

for j=1,…,n𝑗1…𝑛j=1,\ldots,n. This yields that

|β1|=|x−mn​y|≤1c1​yn−1subscript𝛽1𝑥𝑛𝑚𝑦1subscript𝑐1superscript𝑦𝑛1|\beta_{1}|=|x-\sqrt[n]{m}\ y|\leq\frac{1}{c_{1}\ y^{n-1}}

that is

|mn−xy|≤1c1​yn𝑛𝑚𝑥𝑦1subscript𝑐1superscript𝑦𝑛\left|\sqrt[n]{m}-\frac{x}{y}\right|\leq\frac{1}{c_{1}\ y^{n}} (3)

with

c1=(mn)n−1​c2,where​c2=∏j=2n|Im​(ζj−1)|.formulae-sequencesubscript𝑐1superscript𝑛𝑚𝑛1subscript𝑐2wheresubscript𝑐2superscriptsubscriptproduct𝑗2𝑛Imsuperscript𝜁𝑗1c_{1}=(\sqrt[n]{m})^{n-1}c_{2},\;\;{\rm where}\;\;c_{2}=\prod_{j=2}^{n}|{\rm Im}(\zeta^{j-1})|.

Thus we arrived at the crucial point of the method of [8]. Obviously (x,y)=1𝑥𝑦1(x,y)=1. If the upper estimate on the right hand side of (3) satisfies

1c1​yn<12​y21subscript𝑐1superscript𝑦𝑛12superscript𝑦2\frac{1}{c_{1}\ y^{n}}\ <\frac{1}{2y^{2}}

that is

y>(2​c1)1/(n−2),𝑦superscript2subscript𝑐11𝑛2y>(2c_{1})^{1/(n-2)}, (4)

then (3) implies

|mn−xy|<12​y2𝑛𝑚𝑥𝑦12superscript𝑦2\left|\sqrt[n]{m}-\frac{x}{y}\right|<\frac{1}{2\ y^{2}}

and appling Legenre’s theorem we confer that x/y𝑥𝑦x/y is a convergent hj/kjsubscriptℎ𝑗subscript𝑘𝑗h_{j}/k_{j} to mn𝑛𝑚\sqrt[n]{m}, that is x=hj,y=kjformulae-sequence𝑥subscriptℎ𝑗𝑦subscript𝑘𝑗x=h_{j},y=k_{j}. For the corresponding partial quotients ajsubscript𝑎𝑗a_{j} it is well known that

1(aj+1+2)​kj2<|mn−xy|.1subscript𝑎𝑗12superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑗2𝑛𝑚𝑥𝑦\frac{1}{(a_{j+1}+2)k_{j}^{2}}<\left|\sqrt[n]{m}-\frac{x}{y}\right|.

Let A=max1≤i≤s⁡ai𝐴subscript1𝑖𝑠subscript𝑎𝑖A=\max_{1\leq i\leq s}a_{i} where kssubscript𝑘𝑠k_{s} is the first denominator of a partial quotient exceeding C𝐶C. Combining the above estimate with (3) we get

1(A+2)​kj2<|mn−xy|≤1c1​kjn1𝐴2superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑗2𝑛𝑚𝑥𝑦1subscript𝑐1superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑗𝑛\frac{1}{(A+2)k_{j}^{2}}<\left|\sqrt[n]{m}-\frac{x}{y}\right|\leq\frac{1}{c_{1}\ k_{j}^{n}}

whence we obtain

y=kj<c3=(c1​(A+2))n−2.𝑦subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝑐3superscriptsubscript𝑐1𝐴2𝑛2y=k_{j}<c_{3}=(c_{1}(A+2))^{n-2}.

We calculate all denominators of partial quotients up to C𝐶C, we take their maximum, calculate the above bound and check all possible x=hj,y=kjformulae-sequence𝑥subscriptℎ𝑗𝑦subscript𝑘𝑗x=h_{j},y=k_{j} for kj<c3subscript𝑘𝑗subscript𝑐3k_{j}<c_{3} running again the continued fraction algorithm.

Now we return to the validity of (4). Simple calculation shows that (4) is satisfied if

y>(2c2)1n−2​m−n−1n​(n−2).𝑦superscript2subscript𝑐21𝑛2superscript𝑚𝑛1𝑛𝑛2y>\left(\frac{2}{c_{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n-2}}\ m^{-\frac{n-1}{n(n-2)}}. (5)

If m𝑚m is large enough, then condition (5) is satisfied for y≥1𝑦1y\geq 1. Some small values of y𝑦y must be tested for the following values of m𝑚m:

n32≤m≤452≤m≤1072≤m≤29112≤m≤314132≤m≤1078172≤m≤13489192≤m≤48699232≤m≤652798292≤m≤7960210missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression𝑛missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression32𝑚4missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression52𝑚10missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression72𝑚29missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression112𝑚314missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression132𝑚1078missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression172𝑚13489missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression192𝑚48699missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression232𝑚652798missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression292𝑚7960210\begin{array}[]{|c|c|}\hline\cr n&\\ \hline\cr 3&2\leq m\leq 4\\ \hline\cr 5&2\leq m\leq 10\\ \hline\cr 7&2\leq m\leq 29\\ \hline\cr 11&2\leq m\leq 314\\ \hline\cr 13&2\leq m\leq 1078\\ \hline\cr 17&2\leq m\leq 13489\\ \hline\cr 19&2\leq m\leq 48699\\ \hline\cr 23&2\leq m\leq 652798\\ \hline\cr 29&2\leq m\leq 7960210\\ \hline\cr\end{array}

For all these values of m𝑚m we have to test all y𝑦y with

y≤(2c2)1n−2​m−n−1n​(n−2).𝑦superscript2subscript𝑐21𝑛2superscript𝑚𝑛1𝑛𝑛2y\leq\left(\frac{2}{c_{2}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n-2}}\ m^{-\frac{n-1}{n(n-2)}}.

Easy calculation shows that this merely yields testing y=1𝑦1y=1 for the values of m𝑚m contained in the table. This can be done very fast.

Remark
The case n=4𝑛4n=4 was considered in [6]. Remark that in that case we may assume x>0,y>0formulae-sequence𝑥0𝑦0x>0,y>0. We have

|x−m4​y|≤1,|x±i​m4​y|≥m4​y,formulae-sequence𝑥4𝑚𝑦1plus-or-minus𝑥𝑖4𝑚𝑦4𝑚𝑦|x-\sqrt[4]{m}y|\leq 1,\;\;|x\pm i\sqrt[4]{m}y|\geq\sqrt[4]{m}y,\;\;

therefore

|x+m4​y|≥2​m4​y−1≥m4​y𝑥4𝑚𝑦24𝑚𝑦14𝑚𝑦|x+\sqrt[4]{m}y|\geq 2\sqrt[4]{m}y-1\geq\sqrt[4]{m}y

whence

|m4−xy|≤1(m4)3​1y4<12​y24𝑚𝑥𝑦1superscript4𝑚31superscript𝑦412superscript𝑦2\left|\sqrt[4]{m}-\frac{x}{y}\right|\leq\frac{1}{(\sqrt[4]{m})^{3}}\ \frac{1}{y^{4}}<\frac{1}{2y^{2}}

where the last inequality is valid for all y≥1𝑦1y\geq 1.

3 Computational aspects

We were executing the algorithm of [8] with C=10500𝐶superscript10500C=10^{500} for n=𝑛absentn= 3, 4, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, 29 (the case n=4𝑛4n=4 was dealt with in [6]) using the above sharp estimates that made the procedure for binomial Thue equations much more efficient. This efficient algorithm allowed us to perform the calculations for all those 2≤m≤1072𝑚superscript1072\leq m\leq 10^{7} for which the left side of (1) is irreducible.

The procedure was implemented in Maple [4], we used 1200 digits accuracy. For each exponent this calculation involved almost 107superscript10710^{7} binomial Thue equations. The routines were running on the supercomputer (high performance computer) network situated in Debrecen-Budapest-Pécs-Szeged in Hungary under Linux. For each exponent the total running time was about 120-200 hours calculated for a single node which yields a few hours using parallel computing with a couple of nodes.

4 Solutions

In this chapter we list the results of our computation. The triples (m,x,y)𝑚𝑥𝑦(m,x,y) in our table mean that for the m𝑚m there is a solution x,y𝑥𝑦x,y of equation (1). The trivial solution (m,x,y)=(m,1,0)𝑚𝑥𝑦𝑚10(m,x,y)=(m,1,0) is not listed. The solutions are displayed up to sign, that is we include only one of (m,x,y)𝑚𝑥𝑦(m,x,y) and (m,−x,−y)𝑚𝑥𝑦(m,-x,-y).

For each exponent m𝑚m we list the solutions with

max⁡(|x|,|y|)<10500𝑥𝑦superscript10500\max(|x|,|y|)<10^{500}

of all equations with 2≤m≤1072𝑚superscript1072\leq m\leq 10^{7} for which the left hand side of equation (1) is irreducible.

4.1 Solutions for n=3𝑛3n=3

mxy21172192117187198320197263128313710343726341654191921245112651182173215612176125419334271344714221525118151381614172635361426512637289173091813283999101100110113301111332111152123215883531657716172712117291211801736187637319532522196131219813127431412745141​mxy3155443337415133761513605463372431239076344095161409716142916544492332491217149141715080361215514533583118158331816162553685819168601917415392799920180012018615412882762392602119262211970964310647221106492211216623112168231129784721325671313538143613823241138252411411414561440873314706492152531245156242511562625116003126517145361141757526117577261​mxy18745103639189638031944124291968227119684271199272449204218232079755221951281219532812314957224388291243902912611089326999301270013012791091329790311297923113125663232006127432042667213276732132769321335421294343286523485998335936331359383313703710033930334139305341428743514287635144739712453721073460112156466553614665736147307217647964109348627732489494097112506523715065437154871381​mxy548733815781111635931839159320391608531183616307926399940164001401664308126892041168922411723381253740874217408942175866127379506431795084318231387285183441851854418786698252218812189289115134391124451911264519316513639733546197337461991613247100082325710382247110382447110717295210830414331088731914109444287611001757512110591481110593481111169577121117482896112329193411291214531140849721166233427117648491​mxy11765049111868134471235062495124999501125001501126506251513006715231326505111326525111352691543136591103213716036171383033627140607521140609521144703105214887653114887853115456616131564944859157463541157465541158438487916039816331663745511663765511709541112175615561175617561180362113218196317031851925711851945711884611723195111581195113581205378591205380591210645119221242017932142053596215999601216001601217805361621962018132214451212missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression𝑚𝑥𝑦missing-subexpressionmissing-subexpressionmissing-subexpression211missing-sube References

  • [1]
  • [2] A.Bazsó, A.Bérczes, K.Győry and Á.Pintér, On the resolution of equations A​xn−B​yn=C𝐴superscript𝑥𝑛𝐵superscript𝑦𝑛𝐶Ax^{n}-By^{n}=C in integers x,y𝑥𝑦x,y and n≥3𝑛3n\geq 3, II, Publ. Math. (Debrecen), 76(2010), 227–250.
  • [3] M.A.Bennett, Rational approximation to algebraic numbers of small height: The Diophantine equation |a​xn−b​yn|=1𝑎superscript𝑥𝑛𝑏superscript𝑦𝑛1|ax^{n}-by^{n}|=1, J. Reine Angew. Math. 535(2001), 1-49.
  • [4] B.W.Char, K.O.Geddes, G.H.Gonnet, M.B.Monagan, S.M.Watt (eds.) MAPLE, Reference Manual, Watcom Publications, Waterloo, Canada, 1988.
  • [5] I.Gaál, Diophantine equations and power integral bases, Boston, Birkhäuser, 2002.
  • [6] I.Gaál and L.Remete, Binomial Thue equations and power integral bases in pure quartic fields, JP Journal of Algebra, Number Theory and Applications, 32(2014), 49–61.
  • [7] K.Győry and Á.Pintér, Binomial Thue equations, ternary equations, and power values of polynomials, J. Math. Sciences 180(2012), 569–580.
  • [8] A.Pethő, On the resolution of Thue inequalities, J.Symbolic Comput., 4(1987), 103–109.
